article_title: Meta Takes Ofcom to the High Court Over How the UK Calculates Online Safety Act Bills
summary: Submission approvable with 2 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Meta Takes Ofcom to the High Court Over How the UK Calculates Online Safety Act Bills
content_preview.summary: Meta filed a judicial review challenging Ofcom's use of qualifying worldwide revenue to set fees and penalties under the UK's Online Safety Act, with a full hearing scheduled for October.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Meta has launched a judicial review against the UK's communications regulator Ofcom, challenging the methodology used to calculate fees and penalties under the Online Safety Act — a dispute that could force a fundamental recalibration of how the country's digital safety regime is funded. A preliminary hearing was held at the London High Court on May 7, 2026, before Mr Justice Chamberlain, according to [AOL / Press Association](https://www.aol.com/articles/meta-brings-high-court-chal...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Comprehensive, well-sourced coverage of Meta's judicial review against Ofcom. Multiple perspectives covered. Good use of financial stakes to contextualize the dispute.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 7 sources verified. source-0 (TNW): confirmed Meta's public statement about fees and penalties. source-1 (The Register): confirmed £18 million cap, 10% QWR, $201 billion Meta revenue, Ofcom 'plain reading' quote, Meta's statement. source-2 (biometricupdate.com): confirmed October hearing, fees amounts. source-3 (AOL/PA): confirmed Mr Justice Chamberlain, Monica Carss-Frisk KC, 'troubling' court document quote, Epic Games intervention. source-4 (BM Magazine): confirmed October 13 and 14 dates, £164 million tariff revenue, £233 million Ofcom budget, 4chan and Kiwi Farms litigation. source-5 (National Law Review): confirmed 0.02%-0.03% fee rates, £250 million threshold, £10 million exemption, September 2026 invoices, April 11 registration deadline. source-6 (Inforrm): confirmed 8579 LLC fines details, Kick Online Entertainment, motherless.com, crazyporn.xxx, section 12 OSA. Found two attribution/accuracy issues (see corrections).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Two recoverable issues found: (1) Quote 'once a service is offered to British users, the entirety of its global turnover is counted' is attributed to The Register but actually appears verbatim in BM Magazine source; The Register uses different phrasing. (2) Kick Online Entertainment fine stated as £800,000 but Inforrm source shows £800,000 for section 12 breach + £30,000 for info request = £830,000 total. The automated placeholder text warning for 'xxx+' is a false positive - crazyporn.xxx is an actual domain name, not placeholder text.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. Legal dispute covered without taking sides.
editor_notes.originality: Unique coverage of this specific judicial review.
concerns: Quote misattribution: 'once a service is offered to British users' attributed to The Register but comes from BM Magazine
Kick Online fine understated: £800,000 reported vs £830,000 actual total
recommendations: Add bmmagazine.co.uk, natlawreview.com, inforrm.org, biometricupdate.com to allowlist (done)
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Solid article with two minor recoverable issues. Published with corrections record.
